scope.area: HTTP request execution pipeline and response flow

summary.highLevelVerdict: The intended HTTP flow is visible from adapter entry to response writing, but the current implementation has several runtime blockers. The most important ones are lazy adapter loading on frozen gateways, missing tenant-route resolution before body/pipeline use, mutation of frozen RequestData during body parsing, and response cookie/static-file helpers that can throw.

transportEntry.question: Does the network adapter accept HTTP requests and enter the engine flow correctly?
transportEntry.answer: Yes. The active uws adapter creates an SSL app, binds a catch-all HTTP handler, derives client IP, wraps the request in an ExecutionContext, and enters the request flow through the HTTP handler.

evidence.note: uWS SSL app boot and HTTP handler setup.
Catch-all HTTP route creates execution context and dispatches request hooks.

itemsToGrantCorrectness.item: HTTP listener is actively registered
Each incoming request gets an execution context

requestContextAndNormalization.question: Is inbound request data normalized into a stable execution model correctly?
requestContextAndNormalization.answer: Partially. The handler collects URL, query, method, and headers and hands them to ExecutionContext.setupRequestData(). RequestData then normalizes method, URL, headers, query, cookies, and body storage. The weak point is that RequestData freezes itself immediately, but the body reader later mutates requestData.body, which makes the normalized request object incompatible with later body parsing.

evidence.note: Request metadata is assembled before body read.
ExecutionContext creates RequestData and may preload session data.
RequestData normalizes and then freezes itself.
Body parser writes back into requestData.body after RequestData has already been frozen.

itemsToGrantCorrectness.item: Request metadata is normalized into a consistent object
The normalized request object remains writable where later flow requires mutation

routeResolution.question: Is tenant route resolution performed before the pipeline and response logic depend on it?
routeResolution.answer: No. The manager facade exposes resolveRoute(), but the HTTP handler never calls it before body reading, session flow, or pipeline execution. That leaves executionContext.tenantRoute unset even though both the body reader and the request pipeline expect it to exist.

evidence.note: Manager facade exposes resolveRoute().
The live flow goes from getSessionData() directly to runHttpPipeline() without calling resolveRoute().
runHttpPipeline() assumes executionContext.tenantRoute exists and calls isRedirect() on it.
Body read logic also expects tenantRoute to exist for size and upload settings.

itemsToGrantCorrectness.item: Tenant route is resolved before pipeline flow starts
Body parsing has the tenant route context it depends on

bodyReadAndAdmissionControl.question: Are body reads, CSRF checks, and payload size limits structured correctly?
bodyReadAndAdmissionControl.answer: The intended structure is good: the body reader gates mutable methods, checks CSRF, enforces content-length and streaming byte limits, and supports multipart, JSON, urlencoded, and plain text. In practice it is blocked by two issues: it depends on tenantRoute before route resolution has happened, and it mutates a frozen RequestData object.

evidence.note: Only data methods enter the body-read path.
CSRF auth and content-length enforcement.
Multipart path streams data and enforces byte ceilings.
JSON, urlencoded, and text bodies are parsed into requestData.body.

itemsToGrantCorrectness.item: HTTP data methods are separated from no-body methods
CSRF and payload limits are enforced in the body path
The body parser can complete successfully with the current RequestData contract

pipelineExecutionCore.question: Can the request pipeline core execute ordered HTTP stages and complete request timing correctly?
pipelineExecutionCore.answer: The intended stage loop is present, including stage hooks, per-stage metadata, break semantics, pipeline error fallback, and finish callback execution. But the current RequestPipeline gateway freezes itself in the constructor and only lazy-loads its adapter later. Because GatewayCore.loadAdapter() assigns to this.adapter, the first HTTP pipeline call is likely to throw on a frozen object before any stage executes.

evidence.note: RequestPipeline freezes itself during construction.
runHttpPipeline() lazy-loads the adapter at request time.
loadAdapter() assigns to this.adapter, which is incompatible with a frozen instance.
Execution metadata includes currentStageIndex/currentStageName so stage tracking itself is now compatible.
Finish callbacks finalize request timing metadata.

itemsToGrantCorrectness.item: Stage hooks and break/error structure exist
Pipeline adapter can be loaded successfully at request time with current object lifecycle

bundledStages.question: Do the bundled HTTP stages implement a working request progression?
bundledStages.answer: Only partially. The controller stage has the right overall idea, but the bundled stage set has multiple runtime problems. The static file stage calls tryStreamStaticAsset() with the wrong arguments, checks an async storage method synchronously, and calls setStreamResponse(), which does not exist on StageContext. The rate-limit stage is empty. The controller stage can also throw when setting cookies because StageContext assumes responseData.cookie is already an object.

evidence.note: HTTP stage order is queue -> local file stream -> mid rate limit -> tenant controller.
tryStreamStaticAsset() is called without the execution/stage context it expects.
Static streaming helper expects executionContext and calls a missing setStreamResponse() method.
fileExists() is async, but the static stage treats it as sync.
Rate-limit stage is currently empty.
Controller responses are mapped into StageContext response mutators.
setCookie() writes into responseData.cookie without ensuring it is an object.

itemsToGrantCorrectness.item: Bundled HTTP stage sequence is present
Static file stage is currently executable as written
Controller stage can safely map cookies into the response envelope
Rate limiting exists as a pipeline stage

responseAssemblyAndWrite.question: Can the response flow assemble status, headers, cookies, and body and write them back safely?
responseAssemblyAndWrite.answer: The intended writer is sound: it emits response write hooks, optionally adds session cookies, writes status and headers, then supports stream, buffer, object-as-JSON, string, and empty responses. The weak spots are responseData.cookie starting as null while StageContext.setCookie assumes an object, and the writer depending on tenantRoute.session even though tenantRoute may still be unresolved earlier in the flow.

evidence.note: Response envelope initializes cookie as null.
Session cookie response generation can initialize responseData.cookie properly.
Write flow supports session cookies, headers, streams, buffers, JSON, strings, and fallback end().
Write error and end hooks are emitted in catch/finally.

itemsToGrantCorrectness.item: Response writer supports the expected body shapes
Cookie mutation is safe from the first write path onward
Session cookie update path exists

abortAndErrorHandling.question: Are aborts, request errors, and response-write failures handled coherently?
abortAndErrorHandling.answer: Partially. The handler marks aborted responses, the pipeline emits BREAK and ERROR hooks, the top-level HTTP handler returns a 500 for uncaught request flow errors, and the response writer emits BREAK/ERROR/END hooks. The main gap is that some infrastructure mismatches will throw before the intended hook-driven flow can provide graceful behavior.

evidence.note: uWS aborted response is mapped to executionContext.abort().
Top-level catch sends request error hook and 500 fallback if not aborted.
Response write catch/finally dispatches write error, break, and end hooks.

itemsToGrantCorrectness.item: Aborts are observable in the execution context
Unhandled request-flow errors produce a 500 fallback
Current HTTP path reaches these graceful handlers before earlier structural failures

transportSpecificCompatibility.question: Are the helper utilities and side flows compatible with the active uws transport?
transportSpecificCompatibility.answer: Not entirely. The standalone HTTP limiter uses Node-style response APIs like statusCode and setHeader, but the active uws path uses writeStatus and writeHeader. That makes the limiter implementation incompatible with the transport object it is currently handed.

evidence.note: Limiter writes statusCode and setHeader using Node HTTP semantics.
Active transport writes status and headers using uWS response methods.

itemsToGrantCorrectness.item: The rate limiter speaks the same response API as the active transport

mustFix: Resolve tenantRoute before any body parsing, session logic, redirect check, or stage execution depends on it.
Remove the frozen-object mismatch between lazy adapter loading and GatewayCore.loadAdapter(), starting with RequestPipeline and shared services used in HTTP flow.
Make RequestData.body writable during parsing or move body mutation out of the frozen request envelope.
Repair StageContext cookie writes so the first cookie mutation cannot throw.
shouldFix: Repair local-file-stream-stage argument flow, async storage usage, and missing response-stream API integration.
Replace or adapt the HTTP limiter so it uses uWS response methods instead of Node http response methods.
Implement the currently empty mid-rate-limit stage or remove it from the bundled sequence until it exists.
niceToHave: Avoid duplicate or premature session fetches and review whether requestData.ip should be populated directly in RequestData normalization.
Document the intended HTTP lifecycle once the blocking issues are fixed so the code and docs stay aligned.
